questions.prompt: What primarily causes an op-amp's Input Offset Voltage (V_OS)?
options: High-frequency RF noise on the power lines
Imperfect manufacturing symmetry between the internal input transistors
Excessive current drawn by the load
Operating the op-amp beyond its temperature limits
questions.correctAnswer: Imperfect manufacturing symmetry between the internal input transistors
questions.explanation: If the two transistors in the differential pair are even slightly different in physical size or doping, they act as if there is a tiny built-in voltage difference between them.

questions.prompt: If an op-amp has V_OS = 5mV, and it is wired as a non-inverting amplifier with a gain of 100, what will the output voltage be if the input signal is exactly 0V?
options: 0mV
5mV
500mV
It will saturate at V_CC
questions.explanation: The internal error voltage acts exactly like a valid DC signal applied to the non-inverting terminal. It gets amplified by the full gain of the circuit (100 * 5mV = 500mV).

questions.prompt: Why are practical integrator circuits so incredibly vulnerable to V_OS?
options: They have identical input resistors
Because they mathematically integrate the constant DC error voltage continuously over time, causing their output to ramp up until the op-amp hits physical saturation
They use high-value capacitors
They require dual power supplies
questions.correctAnswer: Because they mathematically integrate the constant DC error voltage continuously over time, causing their output to ramp up until the op-amp hits physical saturation
questions.explanation: The integral of a tiny constant 1mV error over 5 seconds is a massive sweeping voltage ramp. Because capacitors block DC, there is no DC negative feedback path to stabilize the circuit.

questions.prompt: What does the symbol α (alpha) represent in the context of op-amp errors?
options: The open-loop voltage gain
The phase margin leading to oscillation
The thermal drift: how the offset voltage (V_OS) changes per degree Celsius (ΔV_OS/ΔT)
The ratio of input bias current to input offset current
questions.correctAnswer: The thermal drift: how the offset voltage (V_OS) changes per degree Celsius (ΔV_OS/ΔT)
questions.explanation: Since transistors are highly temperature-dependent, the mismatch between two transistors won't stay static as the silicon warms up. α quantifies this drift.

options: The current required to power the IC chip itself from V_CC
The tiny unavoidable DC current that internal amplifier transistors must physically draw to turn on and remain in their active linear region
The maximum current the op-amp can output to a speaker
The current drawn by the feedback resistor
questions.correctAnswer: The tiny unavoidable DC current that internal amplifier transistors must physically draw to turn on and remain in their active linear region
questions.explanation: Specifically in Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) op-amps, the input pins are actually the Base terminals of transistors. A BJT literally cannot operate without a DC base current.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between Input Bias Current (I_B) and Input Offset Current (I_OS)?
options: I_OS happens at DC, I_B happens at AC
I_B is the average current drawn by the two input pins, while I_OS is the difference between them due to manufacturing mismatches
They are identical names for the same thing
I_OS causes heat, I_B causes noise
questions.correctAnswer: I_B is the average current drawn by the two input pins, while I_OS is the difference between them due to manufacturing mismatches
questions.explanation: Input Bias Current is the average: I_B = (I_B+ + I_B-) / 2. Input Offset Current is the absolute difference: I_OS = | I_B+ - I_B- |.

questions.prompt: Why is it dangerous to use a 10 MΩ resistor connected to the input pin of a standard LM741 BJT op-amp?
options: It will cause the op-amp to oscillate
The tiny input bias current flowing through that massive 10M resistor will create a massive voltage drop error right at the input pin (V=IR)
It exceeds the power rating of the resistor
It lowers the input impedance of the circuit
questions.correctAnswer: The tiny input bias current flowing through that massive 10M resistor will create a massive voltage drop error right at the input pin (V=IR)
questions.explanation: Even a tiny 100nA bias current passing through 10MΩ generates a 1.0V voltage drop. The op-amp amplifies that 1.0V error, destroying the intended output.

questions.prompt: How does 'Null Compensation' physically fix the V_OS error issue in analog circuits?
options: A technician turns an external potentiometer attached to specific pins to manually inject adjusting bias currents into the internal transistor stage until the output error says exactly zero
A computer program digitally subtracts the error from the final reading
The op-amp automatically recalibrates itself every time it is turned on by shorting its own inputs
Adding a large capacitor in series with the input
questions.correctAnswer: A technician turns an external potentiometer attached to specific pins to manually inject adjusting bias currents into the internal transistor stage until the output error says exactly zero
questions.explanation: Pins 1 and 5 are specifically wired to the emitters of the input stage current mirrors. Tuning the pot manually unbalances the mirror to perfectly cancel out the manufacturing mismatch.

questions.prompt: Which type of op-amp technology inherently solves the massive Input Bias Current problem found in old BJT models like the 741?
options: Vacuum Tube op-amps
FET-input op-amps (Field Effect Transistors)
Diode-bridge op-amps
Germanium op-amps
questions.explanation: Instead of BJTs which require base current, FETs use an isolated electrostatic gate to control current. Their input bias currents are often less than 50 pico-amps, meaning high input resistors pose no problem.

questions.prompt: If you are using a practical (lossy) integrator to stop DC drift, what new problem did you just create by adding the parallel DC feedback resistor?
options: It reduces the input impedance to zero
It limits the maximum possible output current
It prevents the op-amp from working as a perfect integrator at extremely low frequencies (approaching DC) because it acts like a normal inverting amplifier at those frequencies
It introduces severe thermal drift
questions.correctAnswer: It prevents the op-amp from working as a perfect integrator at extremely low frequencies (approaching DC) because it acts like a normal inverting amplifier at those frequencies
questions.explanation: At low frequencies, the capacitor's impedance is so huge that the parallel resistor dominates. The circuit stops integrating and just acts like a fixed-gain amplifier.
